parentQuestion: A patient presents with bilateral loss of proprioception and vibration sense in the legs, but pain and temperature sensation are intact. The patient has a wide-based, unsteady gait and positive Romberg sign (increased instability when eyes are closed). This pattern suggests selective damage to ___.

options: The ability to see in the dark
The sense of body position — knowing where limbs are without looking
scaffolds.explain: PROPRIOCEPTION is your internal POSITION SENSE. Close your eyes and touch your nose — proprioception lets you know where your finger and nose are. It's your body's GPS.

wrongExplains.0: Night vision involves the eyes. Proprioception is body position sense.
wrongExplains.2: Hearing uses the ears. Proprioception tells you where body parts are.
wrongExplains.3: Taste uses the tongue. Proprioception is position sense.

options: By asking the patient to identify sounds
By placing a vibrating tuning fork on the skin
scaffolds.explain: Doctors test vibration sense by striking a TUNING FORK and placing it on a bony area (like the ankle or toe). You should feel the buzzing vibration.

wrongExplains.0: Sound identification tests hearing. Vibration is tested with a tuning fork on skin.
wrongExplains.2: Pupil reactions test cranial nerves. Vibration uses a tuning fork.
wrongExplains.3: Muscle strength is motor function. Vibration sense is sensory.

scaffolds.q: Do proprioception and vibration travel in the same pathway?
options: No, they use completely separate pathways
Yes, both travel in the dorsal columns (posterior columns)
scaffolds.explain: Both proprioception and vibration travel in the DORSAL COLUMNS (posterior columns) — the back part of the spinal cord. Damage here affects BOTH together.

wrongExplains.0: They actually share the same pathway — that's why they're often affected together.
wrongExplains.2: Proprioception is sensory, not motor. It travels in the dorsal columns.
wrongExplains.3: Pain uses the spinothalamic tract. Vibration uses the dorsal columns.

scaffolds.q: Does pain/temperature use the same pathway as proprioception?
options: Yes, all sensations use one pathway
No, pain/temp uses a DIFFERENT pathway than proprioception
There is only one sensory pathway
scaffolds.explain: Pain/temperature travel in the SPINOTHALAMIC TRACT (anterolateral cord), while proprioception/vibration travel in the DORSAL COLUMNS (posterior cord). Different pathways can be damaged separately!

wrongExplains.0: Different sensations use different pathways — that's clinically important.
wrongExplains.2: Multiple distinct sensory pathways exist in the spinal cord.
wrongExplains.3: Pain definitely has a pathway — the spinothalamic tract.

scaffolds.q: Where are the dorsal columns located?
options: At the front of the spinal cord
At the BACK (posterior) of the spinal cord
scaffolds.explain: DORSAL = BACK. The dorsal columns run along the POSTERIOR surface of the spinal cord. They carry proprioception, vibration, and fine touch.

wrongExplains.0: The front (anterior) contains motor pathways. Dorsal columns are posterior.
wrongExplains.2: Dorsal columns are inside the spinal cord, along its back surface.
wrongExplains.3: The dorsal columns run the length of the spinal cord, not just in the brain.

scaffolds.q: What happens if you lose proprioception in your legs?
options: You can walk normally without any issues
You have trouble knowing where your feet are without looking
scaffolds.explain: Without proprioception, you lose your internal body map. You'd have to WATCH your feet while walking, and you'd be very unsteady with eyes closed.

wrongExplains.0: Walking requires proprioception. Without it, gait becomes unsteady.
wrongExplains.2: Deafness involves hearing. Proprioception loss affects position sense.
wrongExplains.3: Proprioception loss causes coordination problems, not strength gains.

scaffolds.q: What does the Romberg test assess?
options: Whether a patient can stand steadily with eyes closed
scaffolds.explain: The ROMBERG TEST has the patient stand with feet together. If they sway or fall only when eyes are CLOSED (not open), they're relying on vision to compensate for lost proprioception.

wrongExplains.0: Hearing tests use different methods. Romberg tests proprioception/balance.
wrongExplains.2: Memory is cognitive testing. Romberg tests positional stability.
wrongExplains.3: Blood pressure is measured with a cuff. Romberg tests stance stability.

scaffolds.q: What does a positive Romberg sign (falling with eyes closed) indicate?
scaffolds.explain: If the patient is only unsteady with eyes CLOSED, it means vision was compensating for lost proprioception. Closing eyes removes that compensation. Cerebellar damage causes unsteadiness even with eyes OPEN.

wrongExplains.0: Cerebellar damage causes ataxia even with eyes open. Positive Romberg = proprioception loss.
wrongExplains.2: Falling is not normal balance. It indicates sensory compensation was needed.
wrongExplains.3: Inner ear problems cause vertigo. This pattern suggests dorsal column dysfunction.

scaffolds.q: What does bilateral proprioception/vibration loss with intact pain suggest?
options: Selective damage to the dorsal columns specifically
scaffolds.explain: This pattern of DISSOCIATED SENSORY LOSS (some modalities lost, others preserved) localizes to the DORSAL COLUMNS. The spinothalamic tract (pain/temp) is intact. Common causes: B12 deficiency, tabes dorsalis, multiple sclerosis.

wrongExplains.0: Complete injury would affect all pathways. This is selective dorsal column damage.
wrongExplains.2: Sensory loss is definitely abnormal, not normal.
wrongExplains.3: Muscle disease affects strength, not sensory pathways.
